enrichment.seo_title: Recruitee Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Recruitee (now Tellent Recruitee) costs, who it is for, and how its ATS and collaborative hiring tools compare before you book a demo.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Recruitee, now operating under the parent brand Tellent as Tellent Recruitee, is a cloud-based applicant tracking system and collaborative hiring platform launched in August 2015 by co-founders Perry Oostdam and Pawel Smoczyk in Amsterdam, Netherlands, where the company remains headquartered today. The platform was built to help small and mid-sized companies move recruitment out of spreadsheets and email threads and into a single shared pipeline, combining job posting distribution to more than 200 marketplace integrations and job boards, a customizable careers site branded as CareersHub, candidate sourcing, structured interview scorecards, and collaborative hiring workflows that let hiring managers, recruiters, and interviewers comment and rate candidates in one place. Recruitee raised early seed funding in September 2015 and grew as an independent Dutch SaaS company before merging in February 2021 with the Finnish HR software provider Sympa, a deal backed by growth equity firm Providence Strategic Growth. The combined group went on to acquire the French company Javelo and the German company kiwiHR in 2022, after which the parent organization was renamed Tellent, with Recruitee continuing to operate as the Tellent Recruitee product line and reportedly serving more than 7,000 companies globally. As of 2026 the product is sold across three tiers, Start, Advance, and Optimize, each scoped by the number of active job postings, careers site sophistication, and access to AI-assisted features such as an AI Screening Assistant and AI Matching Assistant that increase in usage credits at higher tiers; Optimize additionally unlocks single sign-on, requisition approval workflows, fairness and bias-reduction tools, and business intelligence integrations. None of the three tiers list public per-seat or per-employee pricing on the company website; instead, Recruitee requires a minimum one-year contract and quotes prospects based on company headcount and chosen plan, though monthly payment terms are available and annual prepayment earns a 20 percent discount. Optional paid add-ons available on any plan include SMS texting, WhatsApp-based candidate outreach, an onboarding workflow tool called Journeys, and AgencyHub for managing external recruitment agencies. Recruitee's careers site and candidate-facing pages support localization into 17 languages, letting employers present job listings and application flows to candidates in their local language even though the recruiter-facing interface itself is primarily English. Competing directly with tools such as Greenhouse, Lever, BambooHR, and Workable, Recruitee is generally positioned as a mid-market ATS for growing teams that want an all-in-one hiring platform without the implementation overhead of larger enterprise HR suites, though its lack of published pricing and 2024 shift from user-based to employee-count-based billing has drawn some customer criticism around cost transparency heading into 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Recruitee, founded in Amsterdam in 2015 and now sold as Tellent Recruitee, is a cloud-based applicant tracking system built to give small and mid-sized companies a shared, collaborative hiring pipeline instead of managing recruitment through spreadsheets and email. It combines job distribution, a branded careers site, and candidate pipeline management in a single platform used by more than 7,000 companies globally.

Following a 2021 merger with Finnish HR provider Sympa and further acquisitions in 2022, Recruitee now operates as a product line within the Tellent group, backed by growth equity firm Providence Strategic Growth.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Recruitee centers on collaborative hiring: hiring managers, recruiters, and interviewers can comment on and score candidates within a shared pipeline, supported by structured evaluation forms, knockout questions, and candidate self-scheduling for interviews. The platform distributes jobs to more than 200 job boards and integration partners and offers a customizable CareersHub careers site that can be localized into 17 languages.

Higher tiers add AI-assisted features, including an AI Screening Assistant and AI Matching Assistant with monthly usage credits, an employee referral program called ReferralsHub, advanced custom reporting, and enterprise controls like single sign-on and requisition approval workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Recruitee sells three tiers, Start, Advance, and Optimize, none of which have publicly listed prices; the company quotes cost based on a prospect's employee headcount, chosen plan, and billing period. A minimum one-year contract is required, though invoices can be paid monthly, and paying annually earns a 20 percent discount.

Optional add-ons priced separately from the base plan include SMS texting, WhatsApp-based candidate outreach, an onboarding tool called Journeys, and AgencyHub for managing external recruitment agencies, so total cost can vary significantly between customers with similar headcounts.

faqs.question: Is Recruitee the same company as Recruit CRM or Recruiterflow?
faqs.answer: No. Recruitee, Recruit CRM, and Recruiterflow are three separate, unrelated recruiting software companies despite similar-sounding names.

faqs.question: How much does Recruitee cost?
faqs.answer: Recruitee does not publish per-plan pricing; costs are quoted based on company headcount, chosen plan (Start, Advance, or Optimize), and billing period, with a 20 percent discount for annual payment.

faqs.question: Is Recruitee still called Recruitee?
faqs.answer: The product is now branded Tellent Recruitee after a 2022 corporate rename, though most customers still refer to it simply as Recruitee.

faqs.question: Does Recruitee require a long-term contract?
faqs.answer: Yes, Recruitee requires a minimum one-year contract, though invoices can be paid monthly or annually.

faqs.question: Can Recruitee's careers site be shown in multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, the public careers site and job listings can be localized into 17 languages, even though the internal recruiter dashboard is in English.

faqs.question: What AI features does Recruitee offer?
faqs.answer: Recruitee includes an AI Screening Assistant and AI Matching Assistant, with usage credits that increase on the Advance and Optimize plans, plus AI-assisted email and feedback writing.

faqs.question: Who founded Recruitee?
faqs.answer: Recruitee was founded in Amsterdam in 2015 by Perry Oostdam and Pawel Smoczyk.

faqs.question: What company owns Recruitee today?
faqs.answer: Recruitee is part of Tellent, formed from a 2021 merger with Finnish HR software provider Sympa and backed by growth equity firm Providence Strategic Growth.

enrichment.seo_title: Recruiterflow Pricing and Review 2026: Is It Right for Your Agency?
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An independent look at Recruiterflow's recruitment CRM and ATS, its Platform and AIRA pricing plans, key features, and how it compares for staffing agencies.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Recruiterflow is a recruitment customer relationship management and applicant tracking system built specifically for staffing agencies, executive search firms, and recruitment process outsourcing teams. Founded in 2017 and headquartered in San Francisco, California, Recruiterflow has grown as a bootstrapped company without outside venture funding, reportedly crossing roughly 24 million dollars in annual recurring revenue and more than 50 crore Indian rupees in ARR by 2026 while remaining profitable, according to company statements. The company employs approximately 64 people as of 2026 and has said it plans to roughly double headcount over the following year, serving recruiting agencies across more than 90 countries and describing itself as trusted by over 1,700 recruitment agencies. Recruiterflow combines a candidate and client relationship database with sourcing tools, including a Chrome extension for bulk sourcing from LinkedIn and other sites, multichannel outreach sequences across email and other channels, recruiting automation workflows the company calls Recipes, sales automation for business development, client and team access controls, custom reporting, and API access. In 2026 the company markets its pricing around two tiers rather than the multi-tier structure some third-party review sites still reference from earlier years: a Platform plan priced at 149 dollars per user per month that includes the combined ATS and CRM, automation, sequences, sales tools, and reporting; and an AIRA plan at custom pricing that layers in Recruiterflow's AI recruiting agent capabilities, including an AI notetaker that automatically summarizes calls, an AI candidate matchmaker, job-change alerts, automated field updates, submission and task agents, omnichannel communication syncing, and custom business intelligence dashboards. Recruiterflow emphasizes that both plans include unlimited jobs, candidates, contacts, and companies, and states there are no hidden add-on fees, with all current and future AIRA features bundled into that tier at no extra charge. All plans carry a three-seat minimum, and Recruiterflow offers a 14-day free trial with full feature access, including the ability to run a live outbound campaign and import an existing candidate database before committing. The platform holds a reported 4.8-star rating across more than 10,000 recruiter reviews on third-party review sites. Recruiterflow competes most directly with other recruitment CRM and ATS platforms such as Recruit CRM, Bullhorn, and Loxo, and is generally positioned toward recruitment agencies and search firms that need a combined sales-and-hiring pipeline rather than corporate in-house talent acquisition teams, which more often choose enterprise ATS platforms like Greenhouse or Lever instead.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Recruiterflow is a recruitment CRM and ATS built for staffing agencies, executive search firms, and RPO teams, founded in 2017 and headquartered in San Francisco. The company has grown as a bootstrapped, profitable business without outside venture funding, reportedly serving agencies across more than 90 countries.

Rather than separating sales and recruiting tools, Recruiterflow combines candidate sourcing, client relationship management, and hiring pipeline tracking into one platform, positioning itself specifically against other agency-focused tools rather than corporate in-house ATS platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality includes a combined ATS and CRM, multichannel outreach sequences, a Chrome extension for bulk candidate sourcing, sales automation for new business development, and recruiting workflow automation the company calls Recipes.

Recruiterflow's AIRA plan adds an AI recruiting agent suite: an automatic call notetaker, candidate matchmaker, job-change alerts, automated field updates, submission and task agents, omnichannel communication syncing, and custom BI dashboards, all bundled without extra add-on fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Recruiterflow's Platform plan is priced at 149 dollars per user per month, with monthly or annual billing options and a minimum of 3 seats per account. Both the Platform and AIRA plans include unlimited jobs, candidates, contacts, and companies.

The AIRA plan, which layers Recruiterflow's AI recruiting agent tools on top of the Platform plan, is custom priced and quoted directly by the sales team. A 14-day free trial with full feature access is available before committing to either plan.

faqs.answer: As of 2026, Recruiterflow's Platform plan is priced at 149 dollars per user per month, with a 3-seat minimum, while the AI-enabled AIRA plan is custom priced.

faqs.answer: Yes, Recruiterflow offers a 14-day free trial with full feature access, including outbound campaigns and candidate database import.

faqs.answer: Recruiterflow is built primarily for recruitment and staffing agencies, executive search firms, and RPO teams rather than in-house corporate talent acquisition teams.

faqs.question: Is Recruiterflow venture-funded?

faqs.question: Where is Recruiterflow based?
faqs.answer: Recruiterflow is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and was founded in 2017.

faqs.answer: Yes, all Recruiterflow plans require a minimum of 3 seats per account.

enrichment.seo_title: Recurly Pricing and Review 2026: Subscription Billing Platform Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Recurly costs, who owns it, and how its subscription billing, churn prevention, and revenue recognition tools stack up for growing SaaS and DTC businesses.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Recurly is a subscription management and recurring billing platform founded in September 2009 in San Francisco, California, by Isaac Hall, Dan Burkhart, and Tim Van Loan, and it remains headquartered in San Francisco today with additional offices in Boulder, New Orleans, and London. The company raised early venture funding, including a 1.6 million dollar seed round led by Polaris Partners in 2010, before undergoing a significant ownership change in 2020 when the technology-focused private equity firm Accel-KKR made a majority equity investment, a deal that closed and was more broadly announced through 2021; Recurly has continued operating under Accel-KKR's backing since. Over its history Recurly has also acquired smaller companies including Redfast, Prive, and LeapRev to extend its churn-prevention and analytics capabilities. As of 2026 Recurly reports several hundred employees, with LinkedIn listing the company in the 201 to 500 employee range and other data providers citing figures between roughly 280 and 350. The platform's core product handles automated global subscription billing, flexible plan and pricing configuration, static and AI-powered churn-prevention tools, dunning campaigns for failed payments, support for more than 10 payment methods and more than 20 payment gateways, multicurrency support, and single sign-on on higher tiers. Recurly's published pricing starts with a Starter plan at 249 dollars per month plus 0.9 percent of billing volume, with the first 40,000 dollars of monthly volume included; above that, an All-Access plan is priced at under 1 percent of billing volume but requires a minimum of 1 million dollars in annual billing volume and is billed annually, adding AI-powered churn prediction agents, advanced analytics, payments orchestration, custom reporting, and SSO. A dedicated All-Access for Shopify plan targets Shopify merchants launching subscription products, adding features like customizable bundles, subscribe-and-save flows, prepaid and gift subscriptions, and a hosted, passwordless subscriber portal. Recurly also sells two adjacent products beyond core billing: Engage, an AI-driven retention and churn-prediction tool starting at 1,600 dollars per month billed annually, and RevRec, an automated revenue recognition and compliance product starting at 850 dollars per month billed annually for handling contract modifications, standalone selling price automation, and multi-GAAP compliant revenue reporting. Recurly primarily competes with subscription billing platforms such as Chargebee, Zuora, and Stripe Billing, and is generally positioned toward mid-market and larger subscription businesses with meaningful payment volume, given its percentage-of-revenue pricing model and 1 million dollar minimum for its highest self-serve-adjacent tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Recurly is a subscription billing and revenue platform founded in 2009 in San Francisco, where it remains headquartered. Since a 2020 majority equity investment from private equity firm Accel-KKR, Recurly has continued to grow through acquisitions of companies like Redfast, Prive, and LeapRev.

The platform serves subscription-based businesses that need automated recurring billing, churn prevention, and revenue reporting, positioning itself against competitors like Chargebee and Zuora for mid-market and larger subscription companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Recurly's core billing product covers automated global subscription billing, flexible pricing and plan configuration, dunning campaigns for failed payments, and support for more than 10 payment methods and 20+ payment gateways. Higher tiers add AI-powered churn prediction, payments orchestration, custom reporting, and single sign-on.

Beyond core billing, Recurly sells Engage for AI-driven retention and upsell campaigns, and RevRec for automated, multi-GAAP-compliant revenue recognition, letting subscription businesses handle billing, retention, and accounting compliance within one vendor relationship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Recurly's Starter plan costs 249 dollars per month plus 0.9 percent of billing volume, with the first 40,000 dollars of monthly volume included. The All-Access plan is priced at under 1 percent of billing volume, billed annually, but requires a minimum of 1 million dollars in annual billing volume.

A separate All-Access for Shopify plan targets subscription commerce merchants, while Engage and RevRec are sold as add-on products starting at 1,600 dollars and 850 dollars per month respectively, both billed annually.

enrichment.seo_title: RecurPost Pricing and Review 2026: Social Media Scheduler Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: RecurPost's Starter, Personal, Agency, and Enterprise pricing, recurring-post features, and how it compares for solo marketers and social media agencies.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RecurPost is a social media scheduling and management platform founded in 2016 by Dinesh Agarwal and headquartered in Winder, Georgia, in the United States. The company has grown into a bootstrapped social media tool used by a reported 100,000-plus businesses, with data from Latka indicating it reached roughly 5.3 million dollars in annual revenue and around 60,000 customers by 2024, and founder interviews citing annualized revenue closer to 27 million dollars in more recent reporting; company size estimates place the team at roughly 11 to 50 employees. RecurPost's signature feature, reflected in its name, is recurring or evergreen content scheduling: users can build libraries of approved posts that automatically republish on a set cadence, alongside standard one-off scheduling, a visual content calendar, and a best-time-to-post scheduler. The platform connects to ten networks, including Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, X, Google Business Profile, Pinterest, TikTok, YouTube, Threads, and Bluesky, and includes a composer with a built-in image editor, AI-generated captions and images, hashtag suggestions, and a royalty-free stock image library. For agencies, RecurPost supports separate client workspaces, password-free client onboarding, internal and external approval workflows, white-label analytics reporting, and bulk post scheduling via CSV import with no stated upload limit. As of 2026, RecurPost sells four self-serve and custom tiers, all backed by a 14-day free trial requiring no credit card: Starter at 9 dollars per month for 2 social accounts and 1 user; Personal at 25 dollars per month for 5 accounts with platform-specific post customization; Agency at 79 dollars per month for 20 accounts, 3 users, client workspaces, and white-label reporting; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier for 100 or more accounts with dedicated support. Annual billing saves roughly two months' cost compared with paying monthly, additional social accounts can be added a la carte for 4 dollars per month, and extra teammate seats on the Agency plan cost 20 dollars per month. RecurPost previously offered a permanent free plan but discontinued it, moving fully to paid tiers backed by a trial period instead. The company backs new subscriptions with a 30-day money-back guarantee and supports billing in more than 30 currencies. RecurPost is most often compared with other social scheduling tools such as Buffer, Hootsuite, Later, and Publer, and is generally positioned toward solo marketers, small businesses, and social media agencies managing multiple client accounts who want lower-cost recurring-content scheduling rather than full enterprise social suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RecurPost is a social media scheduling platform founded in 2016 by Dinesh Agarwal and headquartered in Winder, Georgia. It is best known for its recurring, evergreen content libraries, letting users schedule posts that automatically republish on a set cadence rather than needing to be recreated repeatedly.

The company reports serving more than 100,000 businesses and is bootstrapped, with reported annual revenue figures ranging from roughly 5.3 million to 27 million dollars depending on the source and reporting year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beyond recurring content scheduling, RecurPost offers a visual content calendar, best-time-to-post recommendations, bulk CSV upload, and a composer with a built-in image editor, AI-generated captions and images, and hashtag suggestions. It connects to ten networks including Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, X, TikTok, and Bluesky.

Agency-focused features include separate client workspaces, password-free client onboarding, internal and external approval workflows, and white-label analytics reports, making it suitable for teams managing multiple client accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RecurPost no longer offers a permanent free plan, but provides a 14-day free trial with no credit card required across four tiers: Starter at 9 dollars per month, Personal at 25 dollars per month, Agency at 79 dollars per month, and custom-priced Enterprise.

Annual billing saves roughly two months compared with monthly billing, additional social accounts can be added for 4 dollars per month, and extra Agency-plan teammates cost 20 dollars per month; new subscriptions include a 30-day money-back guarantee.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source first-person arena shooter with fast parkour-style movement, built on the Tesseract engine and featuring a built-in real-time multiplayer level editor.
enrichment.seo_title: Red Eclipse 2 Overview 2026: Free Open-Source FPS Game Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Red Eclipse 2 is, how its parkour-focused arena shooter and Tesseract engine work, and why the game is completely free and open source.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Red Eclipse is a free, open-source first-person shooter originally forked from Cube 2: Sauerbraten, developed by an open community of contributors led by developers including Quinton Reeves, Lee Salzman, and Slawomir Blauciak. The project's first stable release, version 1.0 nicknamed the Ides Edition, launched on March 15, 2011, and the game continued receiving numbered updates through the following years, adding modes like King of the Hill and Coop in version 1.3 in 2012 and reaching version 1.6, the Sunset Edition, on December 21, 2017, which was the last release built on the original Cube 2-derived rendering engine. Red Eclipse 2.0, subtitled the Jupiter Edition, represented a major technical overhaul: it moved the game onto the Tesseract engine, a separate, more advanced fork of Cube 2 written by Lee Salzman, and was released on Steam on December 19, 2019, listed there simply as Red Eclipse 2. The Tesseract engine brought substantially improved graphics to the series, including deferred shading, better shadow mapping, and reflection and refraction support, while the game retained its defining identity as a fast-paced, agile arena shooter built around parkour-style movement, letting players run, jump, wall-run, and slide through vertical multiplayer maps rather than relying purely on traditional run-and-gun combat. Like its Cube 2 lineage, Red Eclipse ships with a full real-time, in-game level editor, giving players the same tools the developers use to build and modify maps, and the game supports numerous competitive modes across free-for-all and team-based play. Critically, Red Eclipse is entirely free to download and play, has no premium tier, in-game purchases, or subscription, and is distributed as free and open-source software under the permissive zlib license, meaning its full source code is publicly available for anyone to inspect, modify, and redistribute. It is available on Steam as well as through itch.io and direct downloads from the official redeclipse.net website, and runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux. Because it is a community-maintained open-source project rather than a commercial studio product, Red Eclipse has no corporate headquarters, no disclosed employee count, and no venture funding; development and ongoing updates are driven by volunteer contributors. It is frequently referenced in roundups of the best free and open-source FPS games alongside titles like Xonotic and OpenArena, and its emphasis on movement-based skill and level-editing tools has earned it a small but dedicated competitive and modding community since its debut.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Red Eclipse 2 is a free, open-source first-person shooter forked from Cube 2: Sauerbraten and developed by a volunteer community rather than a commercial studio. Its first stable release launched in March 2011, and the major 2.0 Jupiter Edition, built on the more advanced Tesseract engine, arrived on Steam in December 2019.

The game is defined by fast, parkour-style movement through vertical multiplayer arenas, along with a built-in real-time level editor that gives players the same map-building tools the developers use themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Red Eclipse 2 runs on the Tesseract engine, adding deferred shading, improved shadow mapping, and reflection and refraction support compared with earlier versions. Gameplay centers on agile, skill-based movement, including running, wall-running, and sliding, layered on top of multiple competitive modes.
The included real-time multiplayer level editor lets players create and modify maps directly inside the game, and the title is fully cross-platform, running natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Red Eclipse 2 is completely free to download and play, with no premium edition, in-game purchases, or subscription of any kind. It is released under the permissive zlib open-source license, so its full source code is publicly available for anyone to inspect, modify, and redistribute.

The game can be downloaded through Steam, itch.io, or directly from the official redeclipse.net website at no cost, and ongoing development is funded and driven entirely by volunteer community contributions rather than commercial revenue.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source data visualization and dashboard tool that connects to many databases and APIs for writing SQL queries and building shareable dashboards; acquired by Databricks in 2020.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source for self-hosted deployments; the original hosted Redash Cloud service was discontinued by Databricks in 2021.
enrichment.headquarters: Originally Rehovot, Israel; the project is now maintained under Databricks, headquartered in San Francisco, California
enrichment.seo_title: Redash in 2026: Is It Still Free, Maintained, and Worth Using?
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Redash's open-source status after the Databricks acquisition, whether the hosted version still works, self-hosting costs, and core dashboard features explained.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Redash is an open-source data visualization and dashboarding tool created in 2013 by Israeli developer Arik Fraimovich, originally out of Rehovot, Israel, to let teams connect to a wide range of databases and APIs, write and share SQL queries, visualize results as charts, and assemble them into shareable dashboards without needing a dedicated business intelligence team. The project grew an open-source community around it, with its GitHub repository accumulating roughly 28,000 to 29,000 stars, and reportedly counted companies such as Atlassian, Cloudflare, Mozilla, and SoundCloud among its users, alongside a paid hosted version of the software. In June 2020, at its Spark and AI Summit, Databricks announced it had acquired Redash, with the deal's financial terms undisclosed; Databricks framed the acquisition as extending Redash's open-source visualization and dashboard capabilities and integrating a hosted version directly into the Databricks platform, positioning Redash alongside other open-source projects in its portfolio such as Apache Spark, Delta Lake, and MLflow. Following the acquisition, however, Databricks shut down the original hosted Redash Cloud service at app.redash.io on November 30, 2021, with user data permanently deleted roughly a month later, on December 30, 2021. The open-source, self-hosted version of Redash has continued to exist and remains free to deploy, but development has slowed considerably since the acquisition, with the project averaging roughly one release per year from 2022 onward, leading many in the community to describe it as being in maintenance mode rather than active development; some contributors have also formed community-led continuations of the project on GitHub. Because Redash is open source, self-hosting it carries no licensing fee, only infrastructure hosting costs, which most estimates put at roughly 5 to 10 dollars per month for a small to medium instance depending on the chosen database and usage scale; third-party managed-hosting providers separately offer paid Redash hosting plans, typically starting around 49 dollars per month, though these are unaffiliated with Databricks. As of 2026, teams evaluating Redash should treat it primarily as a mature but slow-moving open-source self-hosted BI tool rather than an actively growing commercial product, and many organizations that adopted it for lightweight SQL-based dashboards have migrated toward Databricks' own native dashboarding tools or alternatives like Metabase and Apache Superset for continued active development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Redash is an open-source SQL query and dashboarding tool created in 2013 by Arik Fraimovich in Israel, later acquired by Databricks in June 2020. It lets teams connect to many databases and APIs, write shared SQL queries, and turn results into charts and dashboards.

Databricks shut down the original hosted Redash Cloud service in late 2021, and while the open-source self-hosted project remains free and usable, its development pace has slowed considerably, with roughly one release per year since 2022.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Redash connects to dozens of relational databases, NoSQL stores, and APIs, offering a web-based SQL editor with autocomplete, a chart and visualization builder, and the ability to combine multiple queries into shareable dashboards.

Additional capabilities include scheduled query refresh, alerting on query results, result caching for performance, and user and permission management for teams running self-hosted deployments, typically via Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Redash's self-hosted, open-source version is free to deploy, with only infrastructure hosting costs applying, typically estimated at 5 to 10 dollars per month for a small to medium instance depending on database and usage scale.

The original Databricks-hosted Redash Cloud service was shut down in November 2021, but unaffiliated third-party providers now offer separate managed Redash hosting plans, typically starting around 49 dollars per month.

Broad database and data source connector support
Simple, SQL-first workflow familiar to analysts
Large existing community and long GitHub history
Backed historically by well-known companies as users, including Atlassian and Mozilla
cons: The original hosted Redash Cloud service was shut down by Databricks in November 2021
Development has slowed to roughly one release per year since the acquisition
Self-hosting requires infrastructure setup and ongoing maintenance
Feature development has lagged behind actively developed alternatives like Metabase and Superset
No official commercial support channel independent of the community

faqs.question: Is Redash still free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted, open-source version of Redash remains free to use; only infrastructure hosting costs apply.

faqs.question: Who owns Redash now?
faqs.answer: Databricks acquired Redash in June 2020, and the project remains under Databricks' umbrella, though development has slowed considerably.

faqs.question: Is the hosted Redash Cloud still available?
faqs.answer: No, Databricks shut down the original hosted Redash Cloud service at app.redash.io on November 30, 2021.

faqs.question: Is Redash actively developed?
faqs.answer: Development has slowed significantly since the Databricks acquisition, averaging roughly one release per year since 2022, and the project is widely considered to be in maintenance mode.

faqs.question: Who created Redash?
faqs.answer: Redash was created in 2013 by Arik Fraimovich in Israel.

faqs.question: What can Redash connect to?
faqs.answer: Redash connects to a wide range of relational databases, NoSQL stores, and APIs to run SQL queries and build dashboards.

faqs.question: Are there paid Redash hosting options?
faqs.answer: Yes, some third-party providers offer managed Redash hosting, typically starting around 49 dollars per month, though these are unaffiliated with Databricks.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Redash?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Metabase, Apache Superset, and Databricks' own native dashboarding tools.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source PHP content management system from Germany, giving developers full control over templates, modules, and multilingual website structures.
enrichment.headquarters: Frankfurt am Main, Germany (home of lead maintainer Yakamara Media)
enrichment.seo_title: REDAXO CMS Review 2026: Free Open-Source PHP CMS Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What REDAXO is, who maintains it, its module-based architecture, multilingual support, and why it is completely free under the MIT license.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: REDAXO is a free, open-source content management system built on PHP, originally developed internally starting in 1999 by the German web agency Yakamara, based in Frankfurt am Main, before being released publicly as open-source software in 2004 under the GNU General Public License. In 2016, the project switched to the more permissive MIT license, which it continues to use today, allowing REDAXO to be freely downloaded, modified, and used commercially by anyone without licensing fees. Development is led by Yakamara's core team alongside Friends of REDAXO, a volunteer association of several dozen independent web developers and agencies who contribute modules, translations, and core improvements, making REDAXO a community-driven project rather than a single-vendor commercial product. Technically, REDAXO runs on PHP and stores content in a MySQL or MariaDB database, and it is distributed through GitHub under the redaxo/redaxo repository as well as via Composer for PHP dependency management. Its architecture centers on a modular structure: developers build custom content modules and templates with full control over HTML, CSS, and PHP output, separating layout from content so that non-technical editors can update pages without touching code once modules are built. Core platform capabilities include support for fully multilingual websites and content trees, structured media and file management, a granular, configurable user rights and role system that lets administrators define precisely what each backend user can see and edit, and straightforward installation aimed at professional web developers and agencies rather than end consumers looking for a drag-and-drop site builder. The REDAXO backend interface itself is available in several languages beyond its native German, including English, Spanish, Italian, Swedish, and Portuguese, reflecting the project's roots as a German tool that has since built an international user base, particularly across German-speaking Europe where it remains most widely adopted. Because it is entirely free and open source, REDAXO has no pricing tiers, subscription plans, or paid editions; instead, the ecosystem around it is supported by community contributions, sponsorships, and paid implementation or hosting services offered independently by web agencies that specialize in the CMS, similar to how WordPress or Drupal service providers operate. REDAXO is most commonly compared with other open-source, developer-focused PHP content management systems such as Contao and Neos, and it is generally chosen by freelance developers and small-to-mid-size agencies, especially in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland, who want a lightweight, highly customizable CMS core without the bloat or licensing costs of larger commercial platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: REDAXO is a free, open-source PHP content management system developed since 1999 by the German agency Yakamara in Frankfurt am Main and released publicly as open source in 2004. It switched from the GNU GPL to the more permissive MIT license in 2016.

Development is community-driven, led by Yakamara's core team alongside Friends of REDAXO, a volunteer association of independent developers and agencies that contribute modules, translations, and core improvements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: REDAXO centers on a modular content architecture where developers build custom modules and templates with full control over HTML, CSS, and PHP output, separating layout from content for non-technical editors. It supports fully multilingual websites and content trees, plus structured media and file management.

A granular, configurable user rights and role system lets administrators define exactly what each backend user can see and edit, and the backend interface itself is available in German, English, Spanish, Italian, Swedish, and Portuguese.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: REDAXO is entirely free and open source under the MIT license, with no pricing tiers, subscription plans, or paid editions of the core software.

The surrounding ecosystem is supported through community contributions and sponsorships, while independent web agencies that specialize in REDAXO separately offer paid implementation, customization, and hosting services.

cons: Primarily documented and discussed in German, which can be a barrier for non-German developers

Smaller global ecosystem and plugin marketplace compared to WordPress

faqs.answer: Yes, REDAXO is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers.

faqs.answer: REDAXO is maintained by the German agency Yakamara Media in Frankfurt am Main, together with a volunteer community called Friends of REDAXO.

faqs.answer: REDAXO is built on PHP and uses MySQL or MariaDB as its database.

faqs.answer: REDAXO is aimed at developers and agencies comfortable with PHP and templating, rather than no-code website builders.

faqs.answer: The REDAXO backend is available in German, English, Spanish, Italian, Swedish, and Portuguese.

enrichment.short_description: Podcast hosting and monetization platform offering a free unlimited-storage tier alongside paid plans with dynamic ad insertion, an ad marketplace, and cross-promotion tools for independent podcasters.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium tiered subscription (free Core plan plus paid Growth, Pro, and Enterprise tiers), billed monthly or annually, with a 7-day free trial on paid plans.

enrichment.seo_title: RedCircle Pricing and Review 2026: Podcast Hosting and Monetization Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: RedCircle's free Core plan, Growth, Pro, and Enterprise pricing, dynamic ad insertion, and monetization splits explained for independent podcasters.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RedCircle is a podcast hosting and monetization platform founded in 2018 by Mike Kadin and Jeremy Lermitte, former Uber and Facebook employees, and headquartered in Los Angeles, California. The company raised a 6 million dollar Series A funding round co-led by EPIC Ventures and Refinery Ventures, with participation from investors including SignalFire, Bloomberg Beta, and MathCapital, and has been reported to have raised between roughly 7.5 million and 9.5 million dollars in total funding across different data providers; employee count estimates place the team at roughly 19 to 49 people depending on the source and date. RedCircle differentiates itself from many podcast hosts by offering a genuinely free Core plan that includes one podcast with unlimited storage, bandwidth, episodes, and downloads, distribution to all major listening platforms, access to listener donations and exclusive subscriber content, standard analytics, and eligibility to join the RedCircle Ad Platform once a show reaches 500 or more weekly downloads. The RedCircle Ad Platform is the company's built-in advertising marketplace, offering both host-read and programmatic ad opportunities; podcasters keep 70 percent of revenue from host-read ads and 50 percent from programmatic ads placed through the platform, among the more creator-favorable splits in the podcast hosting industry. Above the free Core tier, RedCircle sells three paid plans as of 2026: Growth, at 19.99 dollars per month or 180 dollars billed annually, which adds support for multiple podcasts, AI video generation, 5 monthly episode transcripts, a cross-promotion marketplace for finding other podcasters to swap audience promotion with, and dynamic ad insertion technology that automatically swaps in new ads on older episodes as they continue to be downloaded; Pro, at 34.99 dollars per month or 360 dollars annually, which adds premium analytics, YouTube distribution, 50 monthly transcripts, and team seats for up to five additional users; and Enterprise, at 119.99 dollars per month or 1,199 dollars annually, which adds a dedicated account manager, comprehensive analytics, and 500 monthly transcripts. All paid plans include a 7-day free trial, and plan changes are handled with prorated billing. RedCircle also supports importing an existing show via RSS feed to migrate from another host without losing episode history. The platform is most commonly compared with other independent podcast hosts such as Buzzsprout, Captivate, and Podbean, and is generally positioned toward independent and semi-professional podcasters who want built-in advertising and monetization tools bundled directly into their hosting plan rather than needing a separate ad sales relationship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RedCircle is a podcast hosting and monetization platform founded in 2018 by Mike Kadin and Jeremy Lermitte and headquartered in Los Angeles. The company raised a 6 million dollar Series A round co-led by EPIC Ventures and Refinery Ventures.
RedCircle is best known for pairing genuinely free unlimited-storage hosting with a built-in advertising marketplace, letting independent podcasters monetize without a separate ad sales relationship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The RedCircle Ad Platform offers both host-read and programmatic advertising opportunities once a show reaches 500 weekly downloads, with podcasters keeping 70 percent of host-read revenue and 50 percent of programmatic revenue. Dynamic ad insertion automatically refreshes ads on older episodes as they continue to be downloaded.

Additional features include listener donations, exclusive subscriber content, a cross-promotion marketplace for finding other shows to swap promotion with, RSS feed import for migrating from another host, and episode transcripts that scale by plan tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RedCircle's Core plan is free and includes one podcast with unlimited storage, bandwidth, episodes, and downloads. Paid plans start with Growth at 19.99 dollars per month, followed by Pro at 34.99 dollars per month and Enterprise at 119.99 dollars per month, each with a lower effective rate on annual billing.
All paid plans include a 7-day free trial, and higher tiers add multiple podcast support, premium analytics, YouTube distribution, team seats, and a dedicated account manager at the Enterprise level.

7-day free trial on all paid plans
cons: Ad marketplace access requires reaching 500 or more weekly downloads
Hosting multiple podcasts requires a paid Growth plan or higher
Higher tiers (Pro, Enterprise) can get costly for hobbyist podcasters
Team collaboration with multiple seats is limited to Pro and Enterprise plans

faqs.question: Is RedCircle really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Core plan is entirely free and includes one podcast with unlimited storage, bandwidth, episodes, and downloads.

faqs.question: How much are RedCircle's paid plans?
faqs.answer: Growth costs 19.99 dollars per month, Pro costs 34.99 dollars per month, and Enterprise costs 119.99 dollars per month, each with a lower effective rate on annual billing.

faqs.question: How does RedCircle monetization work?
faqs.answer: Podcasters can earn through listener donations, exclusive content, and the RedCircle Ad Platform, keeping 70 percent of host-read ad revenue and 50 percent of programmatic ad revenue.

faqs.question: What downloads threshold is needed to join the ad marketplace?
faqs.answer: Shows need at least 500 weekly downloads to become eligible for the RedCircle Ad Platform.

faqs.question: Can I move my existing podcast to RedCircle?
faqs.answer: Yes, RedCircle supports RSS feed import to migrate an existing show without losing episode history.

faqs.question: Who founded RedCircle?
faqs.answer: RedCircle was founded in 2018 by Mike Kadin and Jeremy Lermitte.

faqs.question: Does RedCircle offer a free trial on paid plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, all paid plans include a 7-day free trial.

faqs.question: Where is RedCircle based?
faqs.answer: RedCircle is headquartered in Los Angeles, California.

use_cases.title: Independent podcaster monetization
use_cases.description: Earning ad revenue, donations, and exclusive-content income without a separate ad sales relationship.
use_cases.audience: Independent and semi-professional podcasters

use_cases.title: Multi-show podcast network hosting
use_cases.description: Hosting and managing multiple shows under paid plans with centralized analytics.
use_cases.audience: Small podcast networks and multi-show creators

use_cases.title: Podcast audience growth via cross-promotion
use_cases.description: Using the cross-promotion marketplace to swap audience exposure with other shows.
use_cases.audience: Growth-focused podcasters seeking new listeners

features: 1 podcast with unlimited storage, bandwidth, episodes, and downloads
RAP ad marketplace eligibility at 500+ weekly downloads
Dynamic ad insertion and custom dynamic insertion
Team seats for up to 5 additional users
